title: Latex Paint on Cotton
stainChemistry: Latex paint is a water-based emulsion — pigment + acrylic or vinyl polymer suspended in water. While WET, the polymer is dispersed and the whole stain is water-soluble. Once the water evaporates (15–30 min on fabric, faster in heat), the polymer films and cross-links into a flexible plastic coat. Dry latex on cotton is mechanically embedded in the fiber matrix; water no longer touches it, and removal requires a solvent that re-softens the polymer (methanol, acetone, or specialty latex paint remover — each with tradeoffs).
whyThisWorks: Time is the single biggest factor. If you catch it in the first 15 minutes, cold water + dish soap floods the polymer out of the fiber before it films. Once dry, the sequence is: soften the polymer (methanol or paint-specific solvent), emulsify the softened residue (NSD), flush, repeat. Heat accelerates polymerization — never run a painted garment through a warm wash or dryer until the paint is fully gone.
defaultAssumption: Assume the user may or may not have caught it wet. Home voice prioritizes the wet path (fast, clean win); pro voice handles both.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Is the paint still wet? If yes: water + NSD flush, no solvent needed — latex is water-soluble while wet. If dry: skip to step 3 (solvent softening). Time is the variable — treat the difference as two different stains.

Wet paint only. Cold water flush from the back of the fabric. Apply NSD, agitate with a soft brush. The polymer disperses back into the water. Flush until water runs clear. Repeat if any shadow remains. Done.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent on cloth, dry paint only
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dry paint. Apply methanol (or a paint-specific latex remover) to a clean white cloth. Press-and-tamp on the residue — do not pour. Methanol re-softens the acrylic polymer. Flip cloth frequently. Polymer transfers back onto the cloth.
Immediately after methanol, apply NSD to emulsify the softened polymer. Flush with cool water. Methanol alone leaves an oily polymer residue; the NSD pulls it out cleanly.
Repeat methanol + NSD once if residue persists. If the paint is fully dry and spans >2 cm², or has been through a wash/dryer cycle, escalate — cross-linked cured latex usually needs a professional.

steps: 1. Is the paint wet or dry? Different chemistry.
2. Wet: cold flush from the back + NSD + agitate. Flush until clear.
3. Dry: methanol (or latex-specific remover) on a white cloth, tamp — do not pour.
4. NSD flush to emulsify the softened polymer. Cool rinse.
5. Repeat once. Escalate on dry + large or heat-set.
products: Methanol or latex paint remover (Motsenbocker's Lift Off, Goof Off Paint Splatter)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; no heat drying until residue is gone
warnings: Never heat a garment with residual latex — cross-links the polymer permanently
Methanol is flammable + toxic if inhaled — ventilate the area
Test methanol on an inside seam on dyed cotton — some dyes lift

homeSolutions.agent: Act fast — wet latex comes out clean
homeSolutions.instruction: If you catch latex paint while it's still wet, this is an easy win. The clock matters more than the technique. Once the paint dries (15–30 min), it turns into a flexible plastic film that doesn't want to come out. So: if the paint is still soft / shiny / smearing, you have minutes, not hours.

homeSolutions.agent: Wet paint → cold water + Dawn, from the back
homeSolutions.instruction: Run cold water through the BACK of the fabric for 60 seconds. You'll see the paint rinse away. Then a drop of Dawn dish soap on the spot, work it in with a fingertip, rinse again. Two rounds usually clears fresh latex entirely from cotton. Do NOT use warm or hot water — heat speeds up the cross-linking.

If the paint dried, first pick off any bulk — dried latex on cotton forms a brittle film you can scrape off with a dull butter knife or credit-card edge. Don't pull on the fibers. Get off whatever flakes free, then move to the next step.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry paint → methanol or Goof Off, dabbed on a rag
homeSolutions.instruction: For the remaining dry film: dab methanol (denatured alcohol works; sold at any hardware store) or a paint-remover wipe like Goof Off onto a clean white rag. Press the rag onto the stain, don't pour. The methanol re-softens the dried paint. Flip to a clean section of rag, press again. The paint transfers out onto the rag. Ventilate — methanol has fumes.

After the methanol softens the residue, apply a drop of Dawn and work it in gently with a damp (not soaked) cloth. Rinse cool. The Dawn pulls out the softened polymer that methanol alone leaves behind. Repeat once if a faint shadow remains.

homeSolutions.agent: Air dry, inspect before the dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Air-dry. Inspect in daylight before running a hot dryer. Residual latex sets permanently in dryer heat — that's the one irreversible mistake. If you see or feel anything still there, repeat the methanol + Dawn pass.

If the paint spans a large area, has already been through a washer + dryer cycle, or you can see it's cross-linked into the weave, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Latex paint on cotton, [wet or dry when treated], used [methanol / no solvent] at home, no heat." Pros have stronger paint-removers that aren't shelf-safe at home.

steps: 1. Wet or dry? That decides the whole approach.
2. WET: cold water flush from the back of the fabric, + a drop of Dawn, agitate, rinse. Done in a minute.
3. DRY: scrape off brittle bulk with a credit card.
4. DRY: methanol or Goof Off on a rag, press-and-lift on the residue. Ventilate.
5. Follow with Dawn + damp cloth to pull out the softened polymer.
6. Air-dry, inspect in daylight. Any residue cures permanently in dryer heat.
7. Escalate if paint has been washed/dried or covers a large area.
products: Methanol (denatured alcohol — hardware store) OR Goof Off Paint Splatter / Motsenbocker's Lift Off 4
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the paint's been through a warm/hot laundry cycle, covers more than a few square inches of cured film, or survives two full rounds. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: Never heat a garment with residual paint — dryer heat cross-links latex permanently
Methanol is flammable and toxic to inhale — ventilate, don't smoke nearby
Test methanol on an inside seam first on colored cotton

neverDo: Never apply heat to fabric with residual latex — permanent cross-link
Never pour solvent onto fabric — tamp with a cloth
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates methanol, acetone, and most paint solvents at room temperature
Heat (including warm wash) is the one restriction until residue is gone
Colored / printed cotton: test solvent on inside seam first

materialWarnings: No heat until residue is fully gone
Test solvents on an inside seam first on colored cotton

escalation.when: Large area of dried paint, garment already washed / dried with paint present, or two home rounds without clearing it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Latex paint on cotton, [wet or dry] when first treated, used [methanol / Goof Off / no solvent] at home, no heat applied."

contextQuestions: Is the paint still wet or has it dried?
Has the garment been through a warm wash or dryer since?
What color / brand of paint? (for reference; doesn't change the protocol)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Wet vs dry is the only real fork. Wet = trivial. Dry + heat-set = often pro. Large dry film >10 cm² trends toward professional.
